meta.title: Learn Claude Code
meta.description: 19 chapters, 4 stages, from zero to a high-completion Claude Code-like agent

reference.title: Reference
reference.subtitle: Glossary, architecture maps, and deep dive companion docs.

home.hero_subtitle: 19 chapters across 4 stages, from the minimal loop to a multi-agent platform and external capability bus
home.entry_desc: If this is your first time here, do not jump into random chapters. Decide whether you want the mainline, the stage map, or a chapter jump comparison first.
home.entry_start_desc: Best for first-time readers. Get the smallest agent loop working before you add tools, planning, and context management.
home.entry_timeline_desc: Best if you want the full teaching sequence. You will see how the system grows chapter by chapter.
home.entry_layers_desc: Best if you want to understand why the repository is split into stages before diving into chapter detail.
home.entry_compare_title: Compare Two Steps When You Get Stuck
home.entry_compare_desc: Best if you are already mid-way through the course and want to see exactly what capability one chapter adds over another.
home.core_pattern_desc: Every AI coding agent grows from the same loop: call the model, execute tools, append results. Permissions, memory, tasks, teams, and plugins all extend that same loop rather than replacing it.
home.learning_path_desc: 19 progressive chapters grouped into core loop, system hardening, task runtime, and multi-agent platform stages
home.layers_desc: The goal is not to dump details, but to teach the system in the order a developer can actually absorb and rebuild it.
home.guide_start_title: First Read: Lock In The Core Loop
home.guide_start_desc: If you have not really internalized s01-s06 yet, do not rush into multi-agent or MCP. Build the single-agent loop first.
home.guide_middle_title: Middle Read: Separate The State Layers
home.guide_middle_desc: When task, runtime task, teammate, and worktree begin to blur together, step back and re-check the phase boundaries.
home.guide_finish_title: Late Read: Watch The Platform Boundary
home.guide_finish_desc: From s15 onward, the main question is not just more features. It is how the system boundary expands into multiple workers and external capabilities.
home.message_flow_desc: Watch how messages[] grows during a real agent loop with tool calls and results

version.guide_addition_title: Core Structure Added In This Chapter
version.guide_addition_empty: This chapter mainly connects and hardens existing structures rather than introducing one brand-new major module.
version.guide_focus_fallback: Start with the core input, state, and output relationship before you dive into implementation detail.
version.guide_confusion_fallback: If concepts start to blur, return to the question this mechanism solves and the extra capability it adds over the previous chapter.
version.guide_goal_title: What You Should Be Able To Build Afterward
version.guide_goal_fallback: After this chapter, you should be able to reconnect this mechanism into your own agent system.
version.bridge_docs_intro: These companion pages clarify the concepts most likely to blur in this chapter. Read them when you feel confused, not as prerequisites.
version.bridge_docs_fallback_note: This document is not available in the current locale. Showing fallback:

timeline.title: Learning Path
timeline.subtitle: s01 to s19: build the agent system progressively across 4 stages

layers.title: Architecture Stages
layers.subtitle: Start from the smallest working loop, then add control, durability, background execution, multi-agent coordination, and external capability routing.
layers.guide_start_title: If This Is Your First Pass
layers.guide_start_desc: Start at the first stage. Do not skip the core loop, because every later capability wraps around it.
layers.guide_middle_title: If The Middle Starts To Blur
layers.guide_middle_desc: Check which stage a chapter belongs to first. Then ask whether it is adding safety, state, runtime behavior, or platform surface.
layers.guide_finish_title: If You Want To Rebuild It Yourself
layers.guide_finish_desc: After each stage, you should end up with one real system slice you could implement, not just more vocabulary.
layers.core: Core Loop: build the smallest useful single-agent system first, including tools, planning, delegation, skills, and context compaction.
layers.core_outcome: After this stage, you should be able to build a working single-agent harness on your own.
layers.hardening: System Hardening: move from 'it runs' to 'it runs safely and predictably' with permissions, hooks, memory, prompt assembly, and error recovery.
layers.hardening_outcome: After this stage, you should know how to make the agent safer, steadier, and easier to extend.
layers.runtime: Task Runtime: turn work from session-local planning into durable, background, and scheduled execution.
layers.runtime_outcome: After this stage, you should be able to lift chat-level steps into a durable runtime task system.
layers.platform: Multi-Agent Platform: add persistent teammates, protocols, autonomy, isolated execution lanes, and MCP / plugin capability routing.
layers.platform_outcome: After this stage, you should be able to grow a single agent into a collaborative platform.

compare.title: Learning Path Compare
compare.subtitle: Compare what capability is introduced between two chapters, why it appears there, and what you should focus on first.
compare.selector_title: Choose the step you want to compare
compare.selector_note: This page is designed to explain capability shifts before it throws you into code-level detail.
compare.progression_same_chapter: You selected the same chapter twice. Useful for rereading one chapter, not for studying a capability jump.
compare.progression_reverse: This is a backward-looking comparison. Use it to see which ideas in a later system actually come from earlier chapters.
compare.progression_direct: This is the next natural step in the path. It is the best way to study how the system grows chapter by chapter.
compare.progression_same_layer: Both chapters stay in the same capability layer, so the focus is on making one idea deeper and more complete.
compare.progression_cross_layer: This comparison crosses into a new capability layer, so the important question is how the system boundary changes.
compare.empty_lead: No short chapter thesis was extracted for this chapter yet.
compare.source_diff_note: If you care about implementation detail, read the diff next. If you only care about the mechanism, the learning cards above should be enough.
compare.empty_hint: Choose two chapters first, then inspect what capability the upgrade actually adds.
compare.architecture_note: Read module boundaries and collaboration first, then drop into implementation detail only if you need it.
